text: Partial Vaginectomy for Excision of Rectovaginal Endometriosis. This video will demonstrate the excision of rectovaginal endometriosis after completion of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. We will also describe laparoscopic techniques to perform a partial vaginectomy when the uterus is left in situ. Lastly, we will incorporate surgical techniques and tips in our discussion.

The surgeon performs a partial vaginectomy to excise a three-centimeter rectovaginal nodule. The rectovaginal space is developed to mobilize the rectum below the nodule's lowest margin. The nodule is tagged with a xeropolysorb suture for localization. Using blunt graspers, the margins of the nodule are delineated, revealing involvement only in the posterior vaginal wall. An inverted V-shaped incision is made with monopolar shears, and the nodule is transected using an anzeal bipolar device. Traction and counter-traction techniques ensure complete excision. The vaginal cuff closure begins with a unidirectional barb suture at the incision apex, re-approximating the posterior vaginal wall longitudinally. The rest of the cuff is closed transversely, with modified Richardson stitches placed at the vaginal cuff angles to incorporate the utero-sacral ligaments.

The surgeon performs a partial vaginectomy to excise a five-centimeter rectovaginal nodule located in the left cul-de-sac. The uterus is suspended to the anterior abdominal wall using a temporary suture and sterile IV tubing to optimize exposure. A temporary left oophorepexy is performed to access the left parametrium. Ureterolysis is conducted due to extensive endometriosis in the left ovarian fossa, with the left uterine artery and its vaginal branches temporarily ligated using endoclips. The rectovaginal space is developed, and the rectum is mobilized off the nodule and posterior vagina using sharp and blunt dissection. A Bryski-Navratol retractor is placed in the posterior vagina, and a colpotomy is initiated with monopolar shears. The nodule is nucleated from its attachment using a bipolar device. The colpotomy incision is reconstructed and reattached to the posterior cervix, with a two-layer closure using a unidirectional barbed suture. Endoclips are removed, and hemostasis is confirmed.

Partial vaginectomy for excision of rectovaginal endometriosis can be performed conservatively with the uterus left in situ or after completion of the hysterectomy to allow for excellent exposure. Tagging the nodule vaginally can help delineate the borders to aid in dissection. Temporary suspension of the uterus and ovaries to the abdominal wall can improve exposure when a uterine manipulator is not used. Advanced laparoscopic suturing techniques are necessary to reconstruct the vagina.
